title: Energy stores and transfers
examTip: Energy questions appear on every AQA Physics paper. You need to name stores precisely, describe transfers correctly, and use Sankey diagrams to calculate efficiency. Learn the eight stores and four transfer pathways cold.
introTips: There are exactly eight energy stores AQA expects you to name — and the names matter. 'Kinetic' not 'movement', 'gravitational potential' not 'height energy'.
Energy is never created or destroyed — it only moves between stores or spreads out as heat. This is the law of conservation of energy, and it underpins every energy calculation you'll ever do.
Sankey diagrams are a common 2–3 mark question. The width of each arrow tells you the amount of energy. You'll learn how to read them and calculate efficiency from them here.
intro: Every time you move, eat, switch on a light, or light a fire, energy is doing something — shifting from one place to another, changing form, or spreading out. But here's what physics tells us: the total never changes. Energy cannot be made from nothing, and it never disappears. Understanding where energy is stored and how it moves is the foundation of everything in physics — engines, power stations, diet science, even climate change. AQA tests this on every paper, so getting it right now pays off massively.

keyPoints.heading: The eight energy stores
keyPoints.content: AQA recognises eight distinct energy stores. You need to be able to name them and give an example of each. Kinetic: any moving object — a car, a kicked ball, a running person. Gravitational potential: any object raised above the ground — a book on a shelf, a roller coaster at the top of a hill. Elastic potential: a stretched or compressed object — a spring, a drawn bow, a squashed rubber ball. Thermal: any object with heat — a hot cup of tea, the Sun, your own body. Chemical: stored in bonds — food, fuels (petrol, wood), batteries. Nuclear: stored in the nuclei of atoms — uranium, the Sun's core. Electrostatic: between charged objects — static on a balloon, the charge between thundercloud and ground. Magnetic: between magnets or magnetic materials — two magnets attracting or repelling.
writeBullets: Kinetic — any moving object
Gravitational potential — any object at height
Elastic potential — stretched or compressed objects
Thermal — any object with heat energy
Chemical — food, fuels, batteries (stored in bonds)
Nuclear — stored inside atomic nuclei
Electrostatic — between charged objects
Magnetic — between magnets

keyTerms.def: A way energy can be held — kinetic, gravitational potential, elastic potential, thermal, chemical, nuclear, electrostatic or magnetic
Energy stored in an object because of its height above the ground
Energy stored in a stretched or compressed object

keyPoints.memTip: Use the acronym KGET-CNE-M: Kinetic, Gravitational, Elastic, Thermal, Chemical, Nuclear, Electrostatic, Magnetic. Or make a story: 'A running cheetah (kinetic) leaps off a cliff (gravitational), lands on a spring (elastic), which heats up (thermal), powered by the food it ate (chemical), from a nuclear-powered planet, while lightning (electrostatic) strikes a magnet (magnetic).' Ridiculous but memorable.

keyPoints.heading: The four ways energy is transferred
keyPoints.content: Energy does not just jump between stores — it is always transferred by a specific pathway. AQA recognises four: Mechanically — by a force acting on an object (pushing, pulling, kicking a ball). Electrically — by a moving electric current through a circuit. By heating — energy flows from a hotter object to a cooler one (conduction, convection, radiation). By radiation — by electromagnetic waves (light, infrared) or by sound waves. The key AQA language is to say 'energy is transferred [pathway] from the [store] store to the [store] store'. Practise that sentence structure.
writeBullets: Mechanically — by a force acting on an object
Electrically — by an electric current
By heating — from hotter to cooler object
By radiation — electromagnetic waves or sound waves

keyTerms.def: Energy transferred by a force acting on an object
Energy transferred by a moving electric current
Energy transferred from a hotter object to a cooler one
Energy transferred by electromagnetic waves (e.g. light, infrared) or by sound

keyPoints.memTip: MEHR: Mechanical, Electrical, Heating, Radiation. The 'MEHR' (German for 'more') gives you all four pathways. Practise completing this sentence for different situations: 'Energy is transferred MEHR-anically from the chemical store to the kinetic store.' Once you can do it for ten scenarios you'll never lose this mark.

keyPoints.heading: Conservation of energy — the rule that never breaks
keyPoints.content: The law of conservation of energy states that energy cannot be created or destroyed — it can only be transferred from one store to another, or dissipated (spread out). The total energy in a closed system stays the same, always. This is one of the most important laws in all of science. When you push a book off a table, the gravitational potential energy does not disappear — it becomes kinetic energy as the book falls, then sound and thermal energy when it hits the floor. Track it and you will always find the same total.
writeBullets: Energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transferred or dissipated
Total energy in a closed system is always constant
Energy that seems to 'disappear' has been transferred to thermal energy in the surroundings
Example: falling book — gravitational potential → kinetic → thermal + sound on impact

keyTerms.def: Energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transferred between stores or dissipated
A system where no energy enters or leaves — total energy stays constant

keyPoints.memTip: Write this on a sticky note: 'Energy is NEVER created. Energy is NEVER destroyed. It only MOVES.' Stick it somewhere you'll see every day this week. The moment you believe this rule truly never breaks, every energy question becomes easier — because you know the total is always the same.

keyPoints.heading: Dissipation — where useful energy goes to die
keyPoints.content: In any real energy transfer, some energy always ends up in a store that is not useful to you. This is called dissipation. The most common dissipated form is thermal energy transferred to the surroundings — in other words, heat that warms the air around a machine. A car engine converts chemical energy in petrol into kinetic energy (useful) but also into sound (engine noise) and thermal energy in the exhaust and engine block (wasted). AQA often asks you to identify the wasted energy store and explain why it is less useful. Key point: all energy eventually ends up as thermal energy in the surroundings — it becomes so spread out it cannot be used again. That spreading out is called dissipation.
writeBullets: Dissipation = energy transferred to a store that is not useful
Most common wasted form = thermal energy transferred to surroundings
All energy eventually becomes thermal energy spread out in the surroundings
Dissipated energy is harder to use because it is spread out and at low temperature
Example: light bulb — electrical → light (useful) + thermal (wasted)

keyTerms.def: The process by which energy is transferred to less useful stores (usually thermal energy in the surroundings)
Energy transferred to a store that is not useful for the purpose of the device

keyPoints.memTip: Think of dissipation as 'spreading and fading'. Useful energy is concentrated. Wasted thermal energy is spread thinly through the surroundings — you can't collect it back up. When AQA asks 'why is this energy less useful?', your answer is always: because it is spread out/transferred to the surroundings at low temperature and cannot easily be used again.

keyPoints.heading: Sankey diagrams and efficiency
keyPoints.content: A Sankey diagram is an arrow diagram where the width of each arrow is proportional to the amount of energy. The thick arrow on the left = total energy input. It splits into arrows on the right: one wide arrow = useful energy output, thinner arrow(s) = wasted energy (usually pointing downward). To calculate efficiency, use: efficiency = useful output energy transfer ÷ total input energy transfer. This gives a decimal — multiply by 100 for a percentage. AQA also writes it as: efficiency = useful power output ÷ total power input. A perfect device would have efficiency = 1.0 (or 100%). In reality it is always less, because some energy is always dissipated.
writeBullets: Sankey diagram: arrow width = amount of energy
Thick left arrow = total energy input
Wide right arrow = useful output; thin arrow(s) = wasted energy
Efficiency = useful output energy ÷ total input energy
Multiply by 100 to get a percentage; always less than 1.0 (or 100%) in reality

keyTerms.def: A diagram where arrow width represents the quantity of energy — shows useful and wasted energy transfers
Useful output energy transfer divided by total input energy transfer — always between 0 and 1

keyPoints.memTip: Efficiency = Useful OUT ÷ Total IN. Think UO/TI — 'You Owe the Total Input'. For Sankey diagrams, fat arrow in, fat arrow = useful out, thin arrow = waste. To find wasted energy: wasted = input − useful. Then efficiency = useful ÷ input. Always check your answer is less than 1 (or less than 100% as a percentage) — if it's bigger, you've divided the wrong way round.

tables.title: The eight energy stores
rows: The object speeds up or has more mass
A skier at the top of a slope
The object is raised higher or has more mass
A compressed spring, a drawn bow
The object is stretched or compressed further
A hot cup of tea, engine exhaust gases
The object's temperature rises or it has more mass
More fuel or food is stored
Uranium fuel rods, the Sun's core
Objects become more charged or move further apart
Magnets are moved further apart (repelling) or together (attracting)

1.question: A book sits on a high shelf. Which energy store does it have?
1.correctFeedback: Correct — the book is at height, so it has energy in its gravitational potential store. It would convert to kinetic energy if it fell.
1.wrongFeedback: The book is at height above the ground, so the correct store is gravitational potential. Kinetic needs movement; elastic needs stretching or compression; chemical is stored in bonds like food or fuel.

3.question: A motor uses 500 J of electrical energy and does 350 J of useful work. What is its efficiency?
3.correctFeedback: Correct — efficiency = 350 ÷ 500 = 0.70 (or 70%). The remaining 150 J is dissipated, usually as thermal energy.
3.wrongFeedback: Efficiency = useful output ÷ total input = 350 ÷ 500 = 0.70. If you got 1.43, you divided the wrong way round — efficiency must always be less than 1. If you got 0.35, you divided by 1000 instead of 500.

commonMistakes: Kinetic energy belongs to moving objects — a stationary object has zero kinetic energy, no matter how heavy it is.
Efficiency is always between 0 and 1 (or 0% and 100%). If your answer is greater than 1, you have divided input by output instead of output by input.

All energy eventually becomes thermal energy in the surroundings — not 'destroyed'. If an exam asks where wasted energy goes, the answer is: transferred to the thermal energy store of the surroundings.
Sankey arrows that point downward represent wasted (dissipated) energy. The arrow pointing right (or forward) is the useful output. Make sure you identify the correct arrow before reading off values.
Gravitational potential energy increases with both height AND mass — a heavier object at the same height has more GPE than a lighter one.
examTips: When describing an energy transfer, use full AQA language: 'Energy is transferred [mechanically/electrically/by heating/by radiation] from the [store] store to the [store] store.' This construction is worth marks.

For Sankey diagram calculations: first read off the input and useful output values (from arrow widths or numbers given). Then efficiency = useful output ÷ input. To find wasted energy: input − useful output.
If asked to 'explain why a device is not 100% efficient', say: some energy is dissipated as thermal energy to the surroundings and cannot be recovered.

videoLinks.label: Energy Stores, Transfers and Sankey Diagrams

revisionCardBullets: 8 energy stores: Kinetic, Gravitational potential, Elastic potential, Thermal, Chemical, Nuclear, Electrostatic, Magnetic
4 transfer pathways: Mechanically (by a force), Electrically (by a current), By heating, By radiation
Law of conservation of energy: energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transferred between stores or dissipated
Dissipation: useful energy lost to the thermal store of the surroundings — always happens in real devices
All energy eventually becomes thermal energy spread out in the surroundings
Sankey diagram: arrow width proportional to energy; fat arrow = input; useful arrow points forward; wasted arrow points down
Efficiency = useful output energy ÷ total input energy (multiply by 100 for %)
Efficiency is always less than 1.0 (100%) — some energy is always dissipated
